phase1: Today is [CURRENT_DATE]. ⏰ SESSION & DATA RELEASE STATUS CHECK (MANDATORY FIRST STEP — complete this before any other analysis): (1) List ALL major US, European, and Asian macro data releases and central-bank events scheduled for today [CURRENT_DATE] (include exact release times in UTC). (2) For each release, determine whether it has ALREADY OCCURRED (post-data) or is still PENDING (pre-data). (3) Output a clear status line: If pending: 'PRE-DATA BIAS — [Event Name] pending at [HH:MM UTC] — bias carries LOWER CONFIDENCE until release. Reduce final Phase 6 confidence by at least 1 point per pending Tier-1 event (NFP, CPI, FOMC).' If all released: 'POST-DATA BIAS — All major releases confirmed. Standard confidence scoring applies.' This status line MUST be referenced and reflected in the Phase 6 Confidence score. Output: - Scheduled Events Today: [list each event + release time UTC + status: OCCURRED / PENDING] - Session Status: [PRE-DATA / POST-DATA / MIXED] | Confidence Adjustment: [state penalty if any]  Browse the live web for the latest BOE and ECB policy data. (from OIS pricing or analyst consensus — exact %) - ECB Terminal Rate Expectation: where is the ECB Deposit Facility Rate expected to settle by year-end? (from OIS pricing or analyst consensus — exact %) - Any BOE or ECB speaker comments in the last 48 hours - Which central bank is more hawkish right now and why ⚠ ENHANCED ANTI-HALLUCINATION PROTOCOL (APPLIES TO EVERY DATA POINT IN THIS PHASE): (A) SOURCE CITATION RULE — For EVERY exact figure you report (percentages, price levels, contract counts, barrel figures, yield levels, PMI values, etc.), you MUST append the source domain immediately after the figure in parentheses — e.g., '3.2% (Source: bls.gov)', '$82.5/bbl (Source: Reuters)', '223K (Source: bls.gov)'. Acceptable authoritative sources: bls.gov, eia.gov, federalreserve.gov, ecb.europa.eu, cmegroup.com, investing.com, reuters.com, bloomberg.com, wsj.com, ft.com, lme.com, coinglass.com, glassnode.com, defillama.com, alternative.me, farside.co.uk. (B) DATA UNAVAILABLE RULE — If you cannot confidently cite a named reliable source for a specific figure, output 'Data Unavailable' — do NOT estimate, round, or extrapolate. A missing figure is far less harmful than a fabricated one. (C) FALLBACK RULE — If live web browsing is blocked, returns an error, or yields no usable results for this phase, do NOT guess or hallucinate any numbers. Instead: state 'Relying on macro trend — live data blocked' at the top of your output for this phase, provide only directional trend commentary based on your most recently known context, label every such statement with '(Trend estimate — unverified)', and set the Phase 6 Confidence score to a maximum of 5/10. Fetch and report (only confirmed released data): - UK: CPI (YoY), GDP (MoM/QoQ), Retail Sales (MoM), Average Earnings / Wage Growth (YoY), and Claimant Count / Employment Change — state Actual vs Forecast vs Previous for each (mark "Data Unavailable" if not yet released) - Eurozone: CPI (Flash YoY), PMI (Manufacturing + Services), GDP (Flash QoQ) — state Actual vs Forecast vs Previous for each (mark "Data Unavailable" if not yet released) - Citigroup Economic Surprise Index for GBP and EUR (exact values — positive = beating expectations; state "Data Unavailable" if not found) - Any high-impact data releases scheduled TODAY that are a risk for EURGBP 
⚙ MARKET REGIME IDENTIFICATION (MANDATORY — output this before the compressed summary block): Based on the data you have collected in this phase AND the prevailing macro narrative, identify the current Market Regime from the following categories: (A) 'Inflation Fears' — the market is currently punishing hot economic data; bond yields are rising because of inflation, not growth; rate-cut expectations are falling; 'bad news is bad news' and 'good news is bad news' for risk assets. (B) 'Growth / Recession Fears' — the market is worried about economic slowdown or recession; weak data signals demand destruction, not rate relief; 'bad news is bad news' for both bonds and risk assets. (C) 'Liquidity / Rate-Cut Optimism' — the market is in 'bad news is good news' mode; soft data raises rate-cut probabilities; risk assets rally on weak prints because easier policy is the dominant narrative. (D) 'Mixed / Transitional' — conflicting signals; the regime is unclear or shifting. IMPORTANT: The identified regime MUST be used to correctly interpret every data point in this phase. For example: in regime (C), a weak PMI print should be flagged as potentially BULLISH for risk assets (more cuts), NOT bearish. Output: - Current Market Regime: [Inflation Fears / Growth-Recession Fears / Liquidity-Rate-Cut Optimism / Mixed-Transitional] - Regime Interpretation: [one sentence — how this regime changes the reading of today's data vs face value] Output (bullet points only, no paragraphs): - UK Momentum: [Positive/Negative/Mixed] — top 2 drivers (confirmed data only) - EUR Momentum: [Positive/Negative/Mixed] — top 2 drivers (confirmed data only) - Citi GBP Surprise: [value or "Data Unavailable"] | Citi EUR Surprise: [value or "Data Unavailable"] - Fundamental Upper Hand: [GBP / EUR / Neutral] Compressed summary block (mandatory — paste this into Phase 2): ``` [P3 - ECONOMIC MOMENTUM] - UK Momentum: [Positive/Negative/Mixed] | Top Driver: [data release + beat/miss, confirmed only] - EUR Momentum: [Positive/Negative/Mixed] | Top Driver: [data release + beat/miss, confirmed only] - Citi GBP Surprise Index: [value or "Data Unavailable"] | Citi EUR Surprise Index: [value or "Data Unavailable"] - Market Regime: [Inflation Fears / Growth-Recession Fears / Liquidity-Rate-Cut Optimism / Mixed-Transitional] | Regime Interpretation: [one line] 
- Core Logic: [which economy is outperforming and what it means for EURGBP] - Bias: [Bullish / Bearish / Neutral] ```

📊 FX OPTIONS EXPIRIES CHECK — EURGBP (MANDATORY FOR FOREX SENTIMENT): Browse for any large FX options expiries or option barrier cuts scheduled for TODAY [CURRENT_DATE] at key strike prices for EURGBP. The two primary cut times are: (1) Tokyo Cut: 02:00 UTC — primarily used for Asian-session options. (2) New York Cut (NY Cut): 15:00 UTC — the dominant global cut time, where the largest notional options expire. SOURCES: Check DTCC swap data repository, Reuters IFR Markets options commentary, CME Group FX options data, or Bloomberg FX options scanner. LOGIC: — Large open interest at a key strike acts as a PRICE MAGNET in the hours leading up to the cut — dealers hedge their gamma exposure by pushing price toward the strike. — Once a large strike expires, the 'gravitational pull' disappears and price can move freely — this is often a source of sharp post-cut volatility. — A large option BARRIER (knock-in or knock-out) at a key level can make price action 'sticky' near that barrier — it acts as a WALL rather than a magnet. If no significant expiries are found, state: 'No significant EURGBP options expiries noted for today.' ⚠ ANTI-HALLUCINATION: Do NOT fabricate strikes or notional sizes. If you cannot find confirmed data, state 'Options expiry data unavailable — check IFR Markets or CME for confirmation.' Output additions: - EURGBP NY Cut Options (15:00 UTC): [strike level + notional size in USD/EUR millions, or 'No significant expiry'] (Source: [domain]) | Effect: [Price Magnet at [level] / Barrier Wall at [level] / No notable effect] - EURGBP Tokyo Cut Options (02:00 UTC): [strike level + notional, or 'No significant expiry'] | Effect: [Magnet / Wall / None] - Options Expiry Directional Bias Implication: [Bullish EURGBP — magnet above / Bearish EURGBP — magnet below / Neutral — no dominant expiry] Add to compressed summary block: - Options Expiries (EURGBP): [NY Cut strike + size / 'None significant'] | [Tokyo Cut strike + size / 'None significant'] | Bias Implication: [Bullish/Bearish/Neutral] Output (bullet points only, no paragraphs): - CFTC Net EUR: [contracts + long/short] | Macro Trend Context: [Bullish/Bearish/Neutral macro positioning for EUR] - CFTC Net GBP: [contracts + long/short] | Macro Trend Context: [Bullish/Bearish/Neutral macro positioning for GBP] - COT Comparison: [EUR or GBP has stronger institutional backing — confirm or contradict EURGBP bias] - IG Retail EURGBP: [X% Long / Y% Short] | Contrarian Intraday Signal: [Yes → [direction] / No] - VIX: [level] | Risk Mode: [Risk-On/Risk-Off] - CNN Fear & Greed: [level + reading] - Overall Sentiment Bias: [Bullish/Bearish/Contrarian] — one line reason Compressed summary block (mandatory — paste this into Phase 2): ``` [P4 - SENTIMENT & POSITIONING] - CFTC Non-Comm Net EUR: [contracts + long/short + extreme? Yes/No] | Macro Context: [EUR trend direction] - CFTC Non-Comm Net GBP: [contracts + long/short + extreme? phase6_master_bias:
Today is [CURRENT_DATE]. Synthesize the 5 fundamental research inputs below into a clear EURGBP institutional intraday bias.

Research Inputs:
1. Policy Divergence (BOE vs ECB): [P1_OUTPUT]
2. Yield Differentials (UK Gilts vs German Bunds): [P2_OUTPUT]
3. Economic Momentum (UK vs Eurozone): [P3_OUTPUT]
4. Sentiment & Positioning (COT EUR + GBP + IG): [P4_OUTPUT]
5. Macro & Geopolitical Risk (UK/EU Specific): [P5_OUTPUT]

⚡ STEP 0 — DOMINANT THEME IDENTIFICATION & DYNAMIC WEIGHTING (MANDATORY — complete this before synthesising the 5 pillars): Identify the single Dominant Market Theme driving price action today. Choose the most accurate label: 'Tier-1 Macro Data Release (NFP/CPI/GDP/FOMC)', 'Central Bank Policy Shift', 'Safe-Haven / Risk-Off Flow Event', 'Geopolitical Escalation / De-escalation', 'Institutional Flow Surge (ETF/COT)', 'On-Chain / Tokenomics Catalyst', 'Liquidity / Rate-Cut Repricing', 'Commodity Supply Shock', or 'No Clear Dominant Theme — Weight Equally'. WEIGHTING RULE: The pillar associated with the Dominant Theme should receive approximately 60% of the decision weight. The remaining 4 pillars share the remaining 40% equally (~10% each). OVERRIDE RULE: If a Tier-1 macro event (NFP, CPI, FOMC decision, or equivalent central-bank rate decision) has occurred today or is PENDING today, you MUST explicitly override and disregard short-term retail sentiment signals (IG Client Sentiment, CNN Fear & Greed) for the directional bias — these contrarian retail signals become statistically unreliable around high-impact macro events. State: 'Retail sentiment overridden — Tier-1 macro event active.' Output: - Dominant Theme: [theme label] - Dominant Pillar: [which of the 5 pillars maps to this theme, e.g., 'Pillar 3'] - Dominant Pillar Weight: [~60%] | Remaining Pillars: [~10% each] - Retail Sentiment Override: [Yes — Tier-1 macro event active / No] 
🔄 STEP 1 — MARKET REGIME ALIGNMENT CHECK (MANDATORY): State the current Market Regime: ['Inflation Fears' / 'Growth/Recession Fears' / 'Liquidity/Rate-Cut Optimism' / 'Mixed']. Then verify that your final directional bias logically aligns with this regime: — In 'Inflation Fears': strong economic data = fewer cuts = bearish risk assets / bearish precious metals / bearish bonds. — In 'Growth/Recession Fears': weak data = demand destruction = bearish commodities and risk assets; strong data = relief rally. — In 'Liquidity/Rate-Cut Optimism': weak data = more cuts = bullish risk assets, crypto, and gold; treat 'bad news as good news'. If your bias contradicts the prevailing regime without a specific overriding catalyst, lower confidence by 1 point and explain the contradiction. Output: - Market Regime: [label] - Regime-Bias Alignment: [Aligned / Contradicts regime — reason] 
Instructions:
- Check for contradictions across the 5 inputs. If 2 or more major factors conflict, lower confidence by at least 2 points and state why.
- If all factors align, confirm alignment and justify higher confidence.
- Recommend the best time-window to trade this bias today based on scheduled events and session liquidity.

Output (bullet points only, no long paragraphs):
- Directional Bias: [Strong Bullish / Mild Bullish / Neutral / Mild Bearish / Strong Bearish]
- Confidence: [X/10] — adjusted down if contradictions found
- Bias Type: [Trend-Following / Mean-Reversion / Contrarian]
- Primary Driver 1: [top factor + why it dominates today]
- Primary Driver 2: [second factor + why it matters]
- Dominant Theme: [theme label]
- Dominant Pillar Weight: [~60% to dominant pillar | ~10% each to others]
- Retail Sentiment Override: [Yes — Tier-1 macro event active / No]
- Market Regime: [Inflation Fears / Growth-Recession Fears / Liquidity-Rate-Cut Optimism / Mixed]
- Regime-Bias Alignment: [Aligned / Contradicts — reason]
- Contradiction Check: [list any conflicting signals, or "None — all factors aligned"]
- Invalidation Point: [what specific event or data shift would flip this bias]
- Optimal Trading Window: [e.g., session + time UTC + reason]

Compressed summary block (mandatory — paste this into Steps 2.2 and 2.3):
```
[EURGBP MASTER BIAS - [CURRENT_DATE]]
- Directional Bias: [Strong Bullish / Mild Bullish / Neutral / Mild Bearish / Strong Bearish]
- Confidence: [X/10]
- Bias Type: [Trend-Following / Mean-Reversion / Contrarian]
- Primary Driver 1: [factor + logic]
- Primary Driver 2: [factor + logic]
- Dominant Theme: [label]
- Dominant Pillar Weight: [dominant pillar + ~60%]
- Retail Sentiment Override: [Yes / No]
- Market Regime: [label]
- Regime-Bias Alignment: [Aligned / Contradicts]
- Contradiction: [summary or "None"]
- Invalidation: [specific event or level]
- Optimal Trading Window: [session + time UTC + reason]
```

Using the research data below, write a comprehensive, highly detailed blog post explaining today's EURGBP fundamental bias so anyone reading it can fully understand the market conditions.
